concepts.name: p-Block Elements – General Introduction
concepts.explanation: p-block elements have their last electron entering the outermost p-orbital. Groups 13 to 18 belong to the p-block. Their valence shell electronic configuration is ns²np¹⁻⁶ (except He). The maximum oxidation state is equal to the total number of valence electrons.

concepts.name: Oxidation States in p-Block Elements
concepts.explanation: The group oxidation state (total valence electrons) is most stable for lighter elements. Oxidation states two units less than the group oxidation state become progressively more stable for heavier elements due to the 'inert pair effect'. Non-metals and metalloids exist only in the p-block.

concepts.name: Anomalous Behaviour of First p-Block Elements
concepts.explanation: The first member of each p-block group differs from the rest due to small size, high electronegativity, and absence of d-orbitals. These elements are restricted to a maximum covalence of four (using 2s and 2p orbitals), while heavier elements can expand their covalence using d-orbitals.

concepts.name: Group 13 Elements – Electronic Configuration and Occurrence
concepts.explanation: Group 13 elements (B, Al, Ga, In, Tl) have the general electronic configuration ns²np¹. Boron is a non-metal; Al, Ga, In, Tl are metals. Boron occurs as orthoboric acid, borax, and kermite. Aluminium is the most abundant metal (8.3% of earth's crust).

concepts.name: Group 13 – Atomic Radii Trend (Anomaly)
concepts.explanation: Atomic radius is expected to increase down the group, but Ga has a smaller radius (135 pm) than Al (143 pm). This is due to the poor shielding effect of the 10 d-electrons in Ga, which do not effectively screen the increased nuclear charge.

concepts.name: Group 13 – Ionization Enthalpy Trend
concepts.explanation: Ionization enthalpy does not decrease smoothly down the group. The discontinuity between Al and Ga, and In and Tl is due to the inability of d- and f-electrons (poor screening) to compensate for the increase in nuclear charge. The sum of the first three ionization enthalpies is very high.

concepts.name: Group 13 – Physical Properties
concepts.explanation: Boron is a hard, black, non-metallic solid with a very high melting point. The other members are soft metals with low melting points. Gallium has a very low melting point (303 K) but a high boiling point (2676 K), making it useful for measuring high temperatures.

concepts.name: Group 13 – Oxidation States and Inert Pair Effect
concepts.explanation: Boron and aluminium show +3 oxidation state. Ga, In, and Tl show both +1 and +3 states. The stability of +1 oxidation state increases down the group: Al < Ga < In < Tl. In thallium, +1 is predominant; +3 is highly oxidising. Compounds in +1 state are more ionic than +3.

concepts.name: Group 13 – Electron Deficiency and Lewis Acidity
concepts.explanation: Compounds of group 13 elements in the +3 state have only six electrons around the central atom (e.g., BF₃). They are electron-deficient and act as Lewis acids, accepting a pair of electrons. Lewis acidity decreases down the group. BF₃ readily forms BF₃·NH₃.

concepts.name: Group 13 – Reactivity towards Air
concepts.explanation: Boron is unreactive in crystalline form. Aluminium forms a thin oxide layer (passivation). Amorphous boron and aluminium on heating form B₂O₃ and Al₂O₃. They also form nitrides with N₂ at high temperature: 2E + N₂ → 2EN. B₂O₃ is acidic; Al₂O₃ and Ga₂O₃ are amphoteric; In₂O₃ and Tl₂O₃ are basic.

concepts.name: Group 13 – Reactivity towards Acids and Alkalies
concepts.explanation: Boron does not react with acids or alkalies. Aluminium dissolves in mineral acids and aqueous alkalies (amphoteric). It reacts with dilute HCl to liberate H₂: 2Al + 6HCl → 2Al³⁺ + 6Cl⁻ + 3H₂. With NaOH: 2Al + 2NaOH + 6H₂O → 2Na⁺[Al(OH)₄]⁻ + 3H₂.

concepts.name: Group 13 – Reactivity towards Halogens
concepts.explanation: Group 13 elements react with halogens to form trihalides (EX₃) except TlI₃. The trihalides are covalent and hydrolysed in water. BCl₃, being electron-deficient, is a strong Lewis acid. AlCl₃ is dimerised (Al₂Cl₆) through halogen bridging.

concepts.name: Anomalous Behaviour of Boron
concepts.explanation: Boron is anomalous due to its small size, high ionization enthalpy, and absence of d-orbitals. Its maximum covalence is 4 (it cannot form [BF₆]³⁻). It does not form dimeric halides like Al₂Cl₆. It forms strong Lewis acids and is a non-metal.

concepts.name: Borax – Structure and Properties
concepts.explanation: Borax is Na₂B₄O₇·10H₂O (correct formula: Na₂[B₄O₅(OH)₄]·8H₂O). It is a white crystalline solid. On heating, it loses water, swells, and forms a glassy bead (borax bead test). It dissolves in water to give an alkaline solution: Na₂B₄O₇ + 7H₂O → 2NaOH + 4H₃BO₃.

concepts.name: Borax Bead Test
concepts.explanation: The borax bead test is used to identify transition metals. Borax on heating forms sodium metaborate and boric anhydride. Transition metal oxides form coloured metaborates. Example: CoO gives blue Co(BO₂)₂ bead.

concepts.name: Orthoboric Acid – Structure and Properties
concepts.explanation: Orthoboric acid (H₃BO₃) is a white crystalline solid with a soapy touch, sparingly soluble in cold water but highly soluble in hot water. It has a layer structure with planar BO₃ units joined by hydrogen bonds. It is a weak monobasic Lewis acid: B(OH)₃ + 2H₂O → [B(OH)₄]⁻ + H₃O⁺.

concepts.name: Diborane (B₂H₆) – Preparation
concepts.explanation: Diborane is prepared by: (i) BF₃ + LiAlH₄ in ether: 4BF₃ + 3LiAlH₄ → 2B₂H₆ + 3LiF + 3AlF₃; (ii) 2NaBH₄ + I₂ → B₂H₆ + 2NaI + H₂; (iii) 2BF₃ + 6NaH → B₂H₆ + 6NaF (industrial).

concepts.name: Diborane – Structure and Bonding
concepts.explanation: Diborane has a structure with four terminal B-H bonds (regular 2-centre-2-electron bonds) and two bridge B-H-B bonds (3-centre-2-electron bonds, also called banana bonds). The two boron atoms and four terminal hydrogens are in one plane; the two bridging hydrogens are above and below the plane.

concepts.name: Diborane – Properties and Reactions
concepts.explanation: Diborane is a colourless, highly toxic gas (b.p. 180 K). It catches fire spontaneously in air: B₂H₆ + 3O₂ → B₂O₃ + 3H₂O (ΔH = -1976 kJ mol⁻¹). It is hydrolysed by water: B₂H₆ + 6H₂O → 2B(OH)₃ + 6H₂. It reacts with Lewis bases to form adducts (BH₃·L).

concepts.name: Borazine (Inorganic Benzene)
concepts.explanation: Borazine (B₃N₃H₆) is formed by heating diborane with excess ammonia. It has a ring structure with alternate BH and NH groups, similar to benzene. It is called 'inorganic benzene'.

concepts.name: Borohydrides (Tetrahydridoborates)
concepts.explanation: LiBH₄ and NaBH₄ (borohydrides) are important reducing agents in organic synthesis. They are prepared by reacting metal hydrides with B₂H₆: 2MH + B₂H₆ → 2M⁺[BH₄]⁻ (M = Li, Na).

concepts.name: Uses of Boron and Aluminium Compounds
concepts.explanation: Boron fibres are used in bullet-proof vests and aircraft composites. ¹⁰B is used in nuclear shields and control rods. Borax and boric acid are used in heat-resistant glass (Pyrex), glass-wool, fibreglass, and as flux. Aluminium is used in alloys, packing, utensils, construction, aircraft, and transportation.

concepts.name: Group 14 Elements – Electronic Configuration and Occurrence
concepts.explanation: Group 14 elements (C, Si, Ge, Sn, Pb) have the general electronic configuration ns²np². Carbon is the 17th most abundant element; silicon is the 2nd most abundant (27.7%). Carbon exists as coal, graphite, diamond, carbonates, hydrocarbons, and CO₂. Silicon is present as silica and silicates.

concepts.name: Group 14 – Covalent Radius Trend
concepts.explanation: Covalent radius increases from C to Si, then only a small increase from Si to Pb. This is due to the presence of completely filled d- and f-orbitals in heavier members, which provide poor shielding and do not fully compensate for the increased nuclear charge.

concepts.name: Group 14 – Ionization Enthalpy Trend
concepts.explanation: Ionization enthalpy decreases down the group. Small decrease from Si to Ge to Sn and a slight increase from Sn to Pb is due to poor shielding of d- and f-orbitals and the increase in atomic size. Ionization enthalpies are higher than corresponding group 13 elements.

concepts.name: Group 14 – Physical Properties
concepts.explanation: Group 14 elements are solids. Carbon and silicon are non-metals; germanium is a metalloid; tin and lead are soft metals. Melting and boiling points are much higher than corresponding group 13 elements.

concepts.name: Group 14 – Oxidation States and Inert Pair Effect
concepts.explanation: Group 14 elements show +4 and +2 oxidation states. Carbon also shows negative oxidation states. Compounds in +4 state are generally covalent. Heavier members show +2 state increasingly: Ge < Sn < Pb. Inert pair effect makes Pb²⁺ stable and Pb⁴⁺ a strong oxidising agent.

concepts.name: Group 14 – Reactivity towards Oxygen
concepts.explanation: Group 14 elements form oxides MO and MO₂. CO₂ and SiO₂ are acidic; GeO₂ is acidic; SnO₂ and PbO₂ are amphoteric. CO is neutral; GeO is acidic; SnO and PbO are amphoteric. Higher oxides are more acidic than lower oxides.

concepts.name: Group 14 – Reactivity towards Halogens
concepts.explanation: Group 14 elements form halides MX₂ and MX₄ (except PbI₄). MX₄ are covalent and tetrahedral (sp³ hybridised). SnF₄ and PbF₄ are ionic. Stability of dihalides increases down the group. GeX₄ > GeX₂; PbX₂ > PbX₄. SiCl₄ is hydrolysed; CCl₄ is not.

concepts.name: Anomalous Behaviour of Carbon
concepts.explanation: Carbon is anomalous due to its small size, high electronegativity, high ionization enthalpy, and absence of d-orbitals. It has maximum covalence of 4. It forms strong pπ-pπ multiple bonds (C=C, C≡C, C=O, C=N) and shows catenation.

concepts.name: Catenation in Group 14
concepts.explanation: Carbon has a unique ability to form chains and rings through C-C bonds (catenation). This is due to the strong C-C bond. Catenation decreases down the group: C >> Si > Ge ≈ Sn. Pb does not show catenation. Bond enthalpies: C-C 348, Si-Si 297, Ge-Ge 260, Sn-Sn 240 kJ mol⁻¹.

concepts.name: Allotropes of Carbon – Diamond
concepts.explanation: Diamond has a three-dimensional network with each carbon sp³ hybridised and bonded to four other carbons (C-C bond length 154 pm). It is the hardest substance due to strong covalent bonds. Uses: abrasive, dyes, tungsten filaments.

concepts.name: Allotropes of Carbon – Graphite
concepts.explanation: Graphite has a layered structure with each carbon sp² hybridised (bond length 141.5 pm). Layers are held by van der Waals forces (distance 340 pm). It conducts electricity along the layers due to delocalised π electrons. It is soft and slippery (used as a dry lubricant).

concepts.name: Allotropes of Carbon – Fullerenes
concepts.explanation: Fullerenes (e.g., C₆₀, Buckminsterfullerene) are cage-like molecules formed by heating graphite in an electric arc in an inert atmosphere. C₆₀ has a soccer ball shape with 20 six-membered and 12 five-membered rings. Each carbon is sp² hybridised. It has both single and double bonds.

concepts.name: Thermodynamic Stability of Carbon Allotropes
concepts.explanation: Graphite is the thermodynamically most stable allotrope of carbon (ΔfH° = 0). ΔfH° of diamond is 1.90 kJ mol⁻¹, and of fullerene (C₆₀) is 38.1 kJ mol⁻¹.

concepts.name: Uses of Carbon Allotropes
concepts.explanation: Graphite fibres in composites (tennis rackets, aircraft), electrodes, crucibles. Activated charcoal is used for adsorbing poisonous gases and in water filters. Carbon black is used as a pigment and filler. Coke is a fuel and reducing agent. Diamond is used in jewellery and as an abrasive.

concepts.name: Carbon Monoxide – Preparation
concepts.explanation: CO is prepared by: (i) limited oxidation of C: 2C + O₂ → 2CO; (ii) dehydration of formic acid with conc. H₂SO₄ at 373 K: HCOOH → H₂O + CO; (iii) industrial: C + H₂O → CO + H₂ (water gas).

concepts.name: Carbon Monoxide – Properties and Uses
concepts.explanation: CO is a colourless, odourless, almost water-insoluble gas. It is a powerful reducing agent: Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂. It is highly poisonous due to forming a complex with haemoglobin (300 times more stable than oxyhaemoglobin). CO has a triple bond (C≡O) and acts as a donor in metal carbonyls.

concepts.name: Carbon Dioxide – Preparation
concepts.explanation: CO₂ is prepared by: (i) complete combustion of carbon: C + O₂ → CO₂; (ii) laboratory: CaCO₃ + 2HCl → CaCl₂ + CO₂ + H₂O; (iii) industrial: heating limestone: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂.
concepts.formula_reaction: CaCO₃ + 2HCl → CaCl₂ + CO₂ + H₂O; CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ (heat)

concepts.name: Carbon Dioxide – Properties and Uses
concepts.explanation: CO₂ is a colourless, odourless gas. It forms carbonic acid (H₂CO₃) with water: CO₂ + H₂O → H₂CO₃. It is a weak dibasic acid. It is used in photosynthesis by plants. CO₂ is a greenhouse gas. Dry ice (solid CO₂) is a refrigerant. Uses: carbonated drinks, fire extinguishers, urea manufacture.

concepts.name: Structure of Carbon Dioxide
concepts.explanation: CO₂ is a linear molecule with carbon sp hybridised. It has two C=O bonds of equal length (115 pm) and no dipole moment. It is a resonance hybrid of three structures: O=C=O ↔ O⁺-C-O⁻ ↔ O⁻-C-O⁺.

concepts.name: Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂) – Structure and Properties
concepts.explanation: SiO₂ (silica) is a covalent, three-dimensional network solid. Each Si is tetrahedrally bonded to four O atoms, and each O is bonded to two Si atoms. Quartz is a common form. It is resistant to attack by most acids and halogens but is attacked by HF and NaOH.

concepts.name: Silicones – Preparation and Properties
concepts.explanation: Silicones are organosilicon polymers with (R₂SiO) repeating units. They are prepared from RₙSiCl₄₋ₙ by hydrolysis and condensation. They are water-repelling, have high thermal stability, high dielectric strength, and resistance to oxidation. Uses: sealants, greases, electrical insulators, waterproofing.

concepts.name: Silicates – Structure and Types
concepts.explanation: Silicates have the basic structural unit SiO₄⁴⁻ (tetrahedron). Si is bonded to four O atoms. Silicate units can be linked by sharing corners to form chains, rings, sheets, or three-dimensional structures. Examples: feldspar, zeolites, mica, asbestos.

concepts.name: Zeolites – Structure and Uses
concepts.explanation: Zeolites are aluminosilicates where some Si atoms in the SiO₂ network are replaced by Al, creating a negative charge balanced by cations (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺). They are used as catalysts (e.g., ZSM-5 for converting alcohols to gasoline) and as ion exchangers in water softening.
